is the one in feftr" (XXXV. 7). This shows that the
author is carried away by his zeal for delineating the theme, and
forgets the grammatical niceties. There are many other violations of
Paninian rules such as in and f«nui, which are out of place
in this appreciation of the master's genius. Many a time the author
had to disregard grammatical rules for the sake of metre.
His Scholarship : His mastery over various Sastras becomes
apparent when we go through this vast Book of Knowledge. Let us
first of all consider his scholarship of grammar, which again confirms
our conclusion that he had to discard grammatical norms only
under poetic licence. He uses the following scholarly expressions :
